subsystem: OpenSC PKCS#15 private-key directory (PrKDF) decoder. The single function shown, sc_pkcs15_decode_prkdf_entry(), parses one PrKDF record read from a smart card's file system: it builds a tree of ASN.1 template entries, decodes a DER CHOICE describing an RSA/EC/DSA/GOSTR3410 private key, populates a struct sc_pkcs15_prkey_info, allocates a heap copy of it, and attaches that copy to the sc_pkcs15_object out-parameter (obj->data).
provenance: Identified by the OpenSC-specific naming and types: the sc_pkcs15_* / sc_asn1_* API, sc_pkcs15_prkey_info, sc_asn1_entry, sc_copy_asn1_entry / sc_format_asn1_entry / sc_asn1_decode_choice, the LOG_TEST_RET / LOG_FUNC_RETURN macros, SC_PKCS15_TYPE_PRKEY_* constants, and the u8 typedef. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_3176_vuln' and the filename train_0090_cwe672.c label this as a training sample; the code matches OpenSC's src/libopensc/pkcs15-prkey.c sc_pkcs15_decode_prkdf_entry().
trust_boundary: The untrusted input is the DER byte stream passed as const u8 **buf / size_t *buflen (line 4) and consumed by sc_asn1_decode_choice() at line 92. This buffer is the on-card PrKDF file content, which is fully attacker-controllable: a malicious or malformed smart card (or a MITM on the card channel) supplies arbitrary DER. Every field the decoder writes into 'info' (subject.value/len, path, modulus_length, params, algo_refs, access_flags, usage, key_reference) therefore originates from hostile data, as does the success/error return of the CHOICE decode that drives the cleanup and dispatch logic below it.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. p15card is the card context, obj is the output PKCS#15 object to fill in, and buf/buflen are an in/out cursor over the DER-encoded PrKDF record. buf and buflen are passed by pointer so the decoder can advance them past the consumed bytes. The bytes behind buf are read from the card and are the primary untrusted input for the whole function.

Local state. ctx is dereferenced immediately from p15card->card->ctx (an unchecked chain of pointers). info is the stack-resident key descriptor that will be filled by decoding and later heap-copied. gostr3410_params[3] is a fixed 3-element scratch array for the GOST key parameters. usage_len and af_len capture the destination sizes of the fixed-width info.usage and info.access_flags fields; they are passed to the decoder so it will not write more bits than those members hold.

A family of ASN.1 template arrays, one per attribute group (common key attrs, common private-key attrs, and per-algorithm RSA/DSA/GOST/ECC key/priv attrs), plus the top-level asn1_prkey CHOICE and the supported-algorithms sequence. Each array is sized by a C_ASN1_*_SIZE macro that must equal the number of entries in the matching static template plus a terminator. These arrays are mutable working copies that get their targets wired to fields of 'info' below, then handed to the decoder.

Four wrapper structs, one per key algorithm, each binding the shared obj plus the common-key-attr, common-private-key-attr and the algorithm-specific private-key-attr template arrays. These become the parameter payloads of the four alternatives of the top-level asn1_prkey CHOICE, so the decoder knows how to populate obj and the attribute sub-trees for whichever alternative matches.

Each mutable template array is initialised by copying the corresponding static const template (c_asn1_*). sc_copy_asn1_entry copies entry metadata including the per-entry ASN.1 type and flags (such as SC_ASN1_ALLOC, SC_ASN1_OPTIONAL, SC_ASN1_PRESENT). The flags carried in these copies determine, later, whether the decoder heap-allocates a field (e.g. the subject octet string) and whether the SC_ASN1_PRESENT bit is set on success — which the error-cleanup and dispatch logic keys off.

sc_format_asn1_entry(entry, parm, arg, flags) sets entry->parm (the decode destination), entry->arg (usually a length pointer), and merges flags. Here the four alternatives of the top-level CHOICE are pointed at their per-algorithm wrapper structs, and each per-algorithm private-key-attr entry is pointed at its key-attr sub-array. This builds the nested decode tree: asn1_prkey -> prk_*_attr -> *key_attr.

Per-algorithm field wiring. RSA decodes a path and modulus_length; DSA decodes a path, an inner value sub-sequence and an indirect-protected sub-entry; GOST decodes a path plus three parameter OIDs into gostr3410_params[0..2]; ECC decodes a path and field_length. Note gostr3410_params is indexed 0..2 against its declared size 3, so the fixed array is exactly consumed. All destinations are members of the stack 'info' (or the scratch array), which the decoder will overwrite from the wire.

Common-key attributes: id, a usage bitfield (with its size usage_len so the decoder clamps the bit string), a 'native' boolean, access_flags (with size af_len), and key_reference. The loop then wires up to SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS supported-algorithm reference slots, each pointing at info.algo_refs[i], and finally attaches that sequence as entry 5 of com_key_attr. The loop is bounded both by SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS and by the template terminator ((asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name), and it indexes two distinct arrays with the same i: info.algo_refs[] and asn1_supported_algorithms[].

Wires the common-private-key subject entry: parm is &info.subject.value (a u8* that the decoder fills), arg is &info.subject.len. The static template c_asn1_com_prkey_attr[0] carries the subject as an OCTET STRING with the SC_ASN1_ALLOC flag, so when this element is present the decoder heap-allocates a buffer and stores its pointer in info.subject.value, sets info.subject.len, and marks asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].flags |= SC_ASN1_PRESENT. This single allocation is the resource whose lifetime the error-cleanup block below (lines 94-99) tries to manage.

Defaults are established after the wiring but before the decode: the whole info struct is zeroed (so subject.value/len and params.data start NULL/0), key_reference defaults to -1, native to 1, and the GOST scratch array to zero. Zeroing info here matters for the cleanup contract: if the decode allocates subject.value or params.data, those are the only non-NULL pointers, and every early-exit path is responsible for freeing exactly those.

The core decode: sc_asn1_decode_choice parses *buf/*buflen against the asn1_prkey CHOICE and, on success, advances buf/buflen. On failure (r<0) the code tries to release whatever the decoder allocated for the subject: it checks that the subject entry is SC_ASN1_PRESENT and frees asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm. Note the guard tests asn1_com_prkey_attr->flags and asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].flags, which are the same entry, and that .parm was set at line 84 to &info.subject.value — the address of the struct member, not the allocated buffer itself. This block is the security-critical resource-release site.

After the cleanup block, END_OF_CONTENTS is returned immediately, other errors bail via LOG_TEST_RET, and success dispatches on which CHOICE alternative set SC_ASN1_PRESENT. RSA/EC/DSA just set obj->type (DSA additionally marks an indirect-protected path). GOST allocates a keyinfo_gostparams buffer into info.params.data, checks the malloc for NULL, then copies the three decoded parameter words into it. Three assert() statements enforce that modulus_length and params.len were zero and that the struct size matches params.len — these are only active in debug builds.

Path resolution. If there is no application AID, the decoded relative path is made absolute against file_app->path; on failure the code frees info.params (the GOST buffer) and returns. Otherwise the app's AID is stored in the path. Importantly, the failure path here frees info.params but does NOT free info.subject.value, even though a subject may have been allocated during the decode.

A backward-compatibility fix-up: cards produced by old OpenSC encoded keyReference as a signed negative value, so any value below -1 is remapped by adding 256 into the intended 0..255 range. This normalises attacker-influenced key_reference but has no memory-safety impact by itself.

If the object is a private key with no auth_id, the code scans obj->access_rules[0..SC_PKCS15_MAX_ACCESS_RULES-1] for a rule whose access_mode matches a private-key usage mode and copies its auth_id. The loop index is bounded by the SC_PKCS15_MAX_ACCESS_RULES constant that matches the array size, and the post-loop check distinguishes 'found' from 'exhausted'. This is defensive and reads only from obj, which is caller-owned.

On success, a heap copy of the whole info struct is made and hung off obj->data; ownership of the embedded pointers (subject.value, params.data) transfers to obj->data via the shallow memcpy. If the malloc fails, info.params is freed and the function returns OOM — but, as on the path at lines 135-140, info.subject.value is not freed on this error path. Finally the subject and path are logged and 0 returned. sc_dump_hex reads subject.value/subject.len as decoded from the wire.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central resource-lifecycle bug (CWE-672 / double free). asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm was set at line 84 to &info.subject.value, i.e. the address of a stack struct member, NOT the heap buffer the decoder allocated into info.subject.value. There are two distinct hazards depending on the decoder's real contract. (1) If free() here is meant to release the subject buffer, then either it frees &info.subject.value (a non-heap, stack address — an invalid free that corrupts the allocator/aborts, and leaks the real buffer), or, if the decoder already released its partially-built allocations on its own error return, freeing again is a classic double free. (2) The buffer info.subject.value is not set to NULL after this free, so it remains a dangling pointer inside the still-live 'info' struct; any later use of info (it is memcpy'd wholesale on the success path) would be a use-after-free. An attacker crafts a PrKDF DER that first decodes the OPTIONAL subject (allocating subject.value and setting SC_ASN1_PRESENT) and then fails later in the CHOICE decode, driving this block on hostile input. The implementation must free info.subject.value (the buffer, not its address), only when the decoder has not already freed it, and must NULL it afterwards.

The two-clause guard is redundant: asn1_com_prkey_attr->flags and asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].flags are the SAME lvalue (the first entry's flags), so the second test adds nothing and cannot be the intended nested check. The visible intent ('if it allocated something, clear it') is only ever gated on the com_prkey subject entry being present, which means the free fires on exactly the condition that a subject WAS allocated — so any lifetime error in the free (previous step) triggers precisely when there is real allocated memory to mishandle. A correct guard would have to distinguish 'the decoder allocated and still owns' from 'already released', which this logic does not do.

Memory leak of the decoded subject on post-decode error paths (CWE-401). If the CHOICE decode succeeded and allocated info.subject.value, and then make_absolute_path fails (attacker supplies a path that cannot be resolved), this path frees only info.params (the GOST buffer) and returns, leaking info.subject.value. The identical omission exists at lines 181-184 (obj->data malloc failure). The invariant: every early return after a successful decode must release BOTH info.params and info.subject.value. This is a resource leak rather than a corruption, so it is lower severity, but it is attacker-repeatable and can pressure memory.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
for (i=0; i<SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS && (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name; i++)
		sc_format_asn1_entry(asn1_supported_algorithms + i, &info.algo_refs[i], NULL, 0);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Latent out-of-bounds hazard tied to constant mismatch (CWE-787/CWE-125). The loop bound uses SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS but it indexes two arrays that are sized by DIFFERENT constants: asn1_supported_algorithms[] is C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE (line 25) and info.algo_refs[] is whatever the struct declares. If SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS ever exceeds C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE (minus terminator) or the algo_refs capacity, the loop reads (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name past the array and formats an entry writing to info.algo_refs[i] past its bound. The terminator check ((asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name) only helps if the array is NULL-terminated before SC_MAX is reached. The implementation must guarantee SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS <= min(elements of asn1_supported_algorithms, elements of info.algo_refs).

Reliance on assert() for a size/heap-safety invariant (CWE-617 / latent CWE-787). The write at lines 126-128 into the malloc'd keyinfo_gostparams buffer is only safe because assert(sizeof(*keyinfo_gostparams) == info.params.len) supposedly guarantees the buffer is exactly struct-sized. In production builds compiled with -DNDEBUG all three asserts are removed, so no runtime check remains: if sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams) ever diverges from what is malloc'd, the field writes overflow the allocation. The invariant must be enforced by a real runtime guard or by construction, not by assert. Severity is low because the sizes are compile-time constants under one control, but it is a fragile contract worth confirming.

summary: This OpenSC PrKDF decoder parses attacker-controllable on-card DER into a stack sc_pkcs15_prkey_info and then heap-copies it to obj->data. The dangerous contract is the decode-error cleanup at lines 94-99: it frees asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm, which is &info.subject.value (the address of the struct member, per line 84), not the allocated subject buffer — an invalid-free / double-free / dangling-pointer hazard (CWE-672) reachable by a card that decodes the OPTIONAL subject and then fails the CHOICE. Secondary issues are subject-buffer leaks on two post-decode error returns and a couple of latent, constant-dependent bounds/assert contracts.

reviewed_anchors.reason: buf/buflen carry the untrusted on-card DER consumed at line 92; entry point for all hostile data.
unchecked pointer chain, but p15card is a caller-owned trusted handle, not attacker-supplied; NULL-deref would be a caller bug not a wire-input bug.
holds the decoder-allocated subject.value and params.data whose lifetime the error paths mismanage.
fixed 3-element scratch array indexed exactly 0..2 at lines 67-69 and 126-128; no attacker-controlled index.
destination-size bounds passed to the decoder to clamp bitfields; they constrain, not expand, writes.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: asn1_*_attr[] arrays sized by C_ASN1_*_SIZE macros (lines 12-25)
reviewed_anchors.reason: template arrays sized to match their static templates; sizing is compile-time and internal, not attacker-driven.
loop bounded by SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS indexes arrays sized by different constants (info.algo_refs and asn1_supported_algorithms) — OOB if constants disagree.
static aggregate initialisers binding template arrays; no runtime length/index logic.
sets up decode targets; the security-relevant consequence (subject alloc) is captured under the info and line-84 anchors.
makes asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm == &info.subject.value, the exact aliasing that makes the line-97 free wrong.
default initialisation; establishes NULL pointers but introduces no unsafe operation.
the untrusted parse whose partial-allocation-then-error behaviour drives the mishandled cleanup.
frees the address of a stack member (or double-frees) — the primary CWE-672 resource-release defect.
clean early return; runs after the cleanup block, adds no new unsafe access on its own.
reads decoder-set flags to set obj->type; index range 0..3 is fixed and matches the CHOICE arity.
size invariant enforced only by assert(); writes at 126-128 overflow if the assert is compiled out and struct size diverges.
frees info.params but leaks info.subject.value on this attacker-reachable error return.
signed compatibility fix-up on a scalar; range remap with no memory effect.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: access_rules search loop bounded by SC_PKCS15_MAX_ACCESS_RULES (lines 158-177)
reviewed_anchors.reason: loop index bounded by the array-size constant with a correct post-loop exhaustion check; reads caller-owned obj.
malloc-failure path frees info.params but leaks info.subject.value (same leak class as lines 136-140); memcpy transfers pointer ownership so the leak matters.
reads the decoded subject with its matching length; on the success path subject.value is a valid buffer, so no over-read given len is the decoder-set length.

findings.title: Invalid free / double free of decoder-allocated subject on the decode-error path
findings.bug_class: Operation on a resource after release (invalid free / double free / dangling pointer)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0090_cwe672.c (and the decoder implementation sc_asn1_decode_choice, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The error cleanup must free the actual heap buffer the decoder allocated into info.subject.value (not &info.subject.value), must do so only when the decoder has not already released it on its own error return, and must set info.subject.value = NULL afterwards so the still-live info struct holds no dangling pointer.
findings.failure_mode: asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm was set at line 84 to &info.subject.value, the address of a stack struct member. When a crafted PrKDF DER causes the OPTIONAL subject to be decoded (allocating info.subject.value, setting SC_ASN1_PRESENT) and the CHOICE decode then fails, this block frees asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm. If that is treated as the buffer it is an invalid free of a non-heap/stack address (allocator corruption / abort) that also leaks the real buffer; if the decoder already freed its partial allocations on error, it is a double free. Because info.subject.value is not nulled, any later shallow copy of info would also expose a dangling pointer. All triggerable by a malicious card.
findings.what_to_confirm: In sc_format_asn1_entry / sc_asn1_decode_choice, confirm what entry->parm holds at free time (address-of vs buffer) and whether the decoder frees allocated OCTET STRING targets on its own error return; confirm whether the historical fix changed this to free(info.subject.value) with a NULL-out.

findings.title: Redundant PRESENT-flag guard cannot distinguish owned-vs-released allocation
findings.bug_class: Faulty conditional guarding a free (redundant expression)
findings.invariant: The guard protecting free() must actually test the condition under which the pointer is owned and still needs freeing here; testing the same flag twice does not encode that.
findings.failure_mode: asn1_com_prkey_attr->flags and asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].flags are identical lvalues, so the conjunction is a no-op duplicate. The free therefore fires on exactly 'subject present' with no additional safety condition, which is precisely when there is allocated memory to mishandle by the primary finding. It also signals the author intended a different (nested) check that was never written.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the second clause was meant to reference a different entry index or a nested attribute entry's flags, and whether correcting it would prevent the free from firing when the decoder still owns/has-freed the buffer.

findings.title: Subject buffer leaked on post-decode error returns
findings.bug_class: Memory leak (missing release of one of two owned allocations)
findings.invariant: Every early return taken after a successful decode must release BOTH decoder-owned allocations reachable from info: info.params (via sc_pkcs15_free_key_params) AND info.subject.value.
findings.failure_mode: If the CHOICE decode allocated info.subject.value and then sc_pkcs15_make_absolute_path fails (line 137) — or the obj->data malloc fails at line 181 — the cleanup frees only info.params and returns, leaking info.subject.value. A malicious card can repeatedly present PrKDF entries with a subject plus an unresolvable path to drive incremental memory exhaustion.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether sc_pkcs15_free_key_params also frees subject.value (it appears to free only params); if not, both error paths (lines 138-139 and 182-183) leak the subject buffer.

findings.title: Supported-algorithms loop bound may exceed backing array sizes
findings.code:
for (i=0; i<SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS && (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name; i++)
		sc_format_asn1_entry(asn1_supported_algorithms + i, &info.algo_refs[i], NULL, 0);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds access from mismatched size constants
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0090_cwe672.c (bound depends on constants defined in headers not provided)
findings.invariant: SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS must be <= the number of usable elements of both asn1_supported_algorithms[] (C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE, minus terminator) and info.algo_refs[].
findings.failure_mode: The loop is bounded by SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS but indexes two arrays sized by unrelated constants. If SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS exceeds C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE, the terminator read (asn1_supported_algorithms + i)->name walks off the array (OOB read); if it exceeds the info.algo_refs capacity, sc_format_asn1_entry wires a write target past that array (OOB write into the stack info struct). This is latent: it is a header-constant contract, not visibly violated in the shown code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Compare the definitions of SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS, C_ASN1_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS_SIZE, and the declared length of sc_pkcs15_prkey_info.algo_refs[] to prove the loop index cannot exceed either array.

findings.title: GOST params heap write guarded only by assert() (removed under NDEBUG)
findings.bug_class: Reliance on disabled assertion for a heap-write bound
findings.invariant: The buffer malloc'd at line 121 (info.params.len bytes) must be exactly sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams) whenever the field writes at lines 126-128 execute — and this must hold in production (NDEBUG) builds where assert() is compiled out.
findings.failure_mode: info.params.len is set to sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams) and the buffer is malloc'd to that size, then the three fields are written through keyinfo_gostparams. The equality is only checked by assert(), which -DNDEBUG removes; if the struct definition and the sized allocation ever diverge, the writes overflow the allocation with no runtime guard. Low severity because both sizes are compile-time constants under the project's control, but the safety argument should not depend on assert.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams) always equals the malloc size across platforms/padding and that no configuration lets params.len be set from the wire; consider a real runtime check instead of assert.

audit_checklist: Determine what asn1_com_prkey_attr[0].parm points to at line 97 (it is &info.subject.value per line 84) and whether free() there is an invalid free, a double free, or leaves info.subject.value dangling; verify the fix frees the buffer and NULLs it.
Check whether sc_asn1_decode_choice frees partially-allocated OCTET STRING targets on its own error return, to know if the line-97 free double-frees.
Confirm sc_pkcs15_free_key_params releases info.subject.value; if not, add subject.value frees on both error returns (lines 138-139 and 182-183) to fix the leak.
Prove SC_MAX_SUPPORTED_ALGORITHMS <= min(usable elements of asn1_supported_algorithms[], length of sc_pkcs15_prkey_info.algo_refs[]) so the line-80 loop cannot go out of bounds.
Verify sizeof(struct sc_pkcs15_keyinfo_gostparams) == info.params.len holds in NDEBUG builds, replacing the assert() at line 124 with a real guard if that equality is not guaranteed by construction.
Confirm the redundant PRESENT guard at lines 95-96 was not intended to test a different (nested) entry, and correct it so the free only runs when the buffer is genuinely owned and unfreed.
